title: Research
text:
Acoustic telemetry + population modeling
Tracking the fate of stocked fish at early life stages is challenging. In collaboration with NYS Dept. of Environmental Conservation, US Geological Survey, Fish and Wildlife Service, and Cornell, we used novel small tags (< 1 g) and a whole-lake acoustic telemetry array to estimate survival of juvenile Cisco (Coregonus artedi) reintroduced to Keuka Lake, NY. We combined these in situ demographic estimates with a population viability analysis. Cisco recovery is limited by high mortality of younger fall fingerling fish upon stocking, likely due to stocking stress and heavy lake trout and avian predation. Older, larger yearling Cisco showed higher survival, but population projections indicated that restoration success will remain limited without improved lake conditions to reduce predation pressure by abundant lake trout. This work provides juvenile survival rates for Cisco, previously difficult to estimate for this species, and offers insights for adapting stocking practices.



Tagged cisco


Data and R code for the population assessment.
eDNA validation
Environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling is a powerful tool for species detection by collecting water samples rather than physically handling organisms. The potential for eDNA transport is well-described in streams and rivers; however, less is known about the accuracy of eDNA in lakes. This study compared Cisco eDNA detections with acoustic telemetry detections and lake currents, measured with economical drifter devices built from parts available at the local hardware store. We found that eDNA successfully detected cisco at coarse scales but less so at finer resolutions. Lake currents could move eDNA as far as several kilometers within 24 hours.



Lagrangian drifter testing


Data and code for the drifter study and the eDNA study.
Food web modeling
Coregonine (Coregonus spp.) restoration has gained attention throughout the Great Lakes basin to rebuild a native prey fish assemblage and increase the resilience of important fisheries. Yet is native fish restoration energetically feasible from an ecosystem perspective? Working with collaborators from the Centre for Ecosystem Management at University of Guelph, we are developing a linear inverse modeling approach to navigate contemporary and future food web scenarios for Lake Ontario and Keuka Lake. This work is currently in progress with funding from NY Sea Grant and the Cooperative Institute for Great Lakes Research.



Cisco-Alewife-Lake Trout food web (fish credit: Edmonson and Eshenroder)

title: About Me
text:
Contact
alk239@cornell.edu
G08 Fernow Hall

I am a Postdoctoral Associate in the Natural Resources and the Environment Section at Cornell University.
I work in fisheries and aquatic science, and am interested in applying quantitative methods from population and ecosystem ecology to address fisheries sustainability. My current research explores food web restoration in the Great Lakes basin.
Before my postdoc position, I completed my PhD in Natural Resources at Cornell and MS in Fisheries Science at Oregon State. I earned my BS in Natural Resources from Cornell.
